# Rust Tutorial Production Notes

This document stores raw notes for a long-form rust lesson video.
It is plain markdown text and intentionally verbose for grep tests.

## Goals
- Explain ownership with practical stories
- Explain borrowing with readable examples
- Explain lifetimes without scary wording

## Audience
- Backend engineers moving from python to rust
- Students who already know basic syntax
- Reviewers preparing subtitles and chapter cards

## Recording Checklist
- Verify microphone level
- Verify subtitle template
- Verify chapter title style

## Segment Catalog

- Segment: Ownership Basics
  Start: 00:00
  Duration: 10 minutes
- Segment: Borrow Checker in Practice
  Start: 10:00
  Duration: 20 minutes
- Segment: Lifetime Patterns
  Start: 30:00
  Duration: 18 minutes

## Voice Tone
Use short sentences and active voice.
Avoid slang and avoid ambiguous pronouns.

## Rendering Constraints
Target frame rate: 30 fps
Target resolution: 1920x1080
Target voice style: calm

## Subtitle Strategy
Break lines at natural pauses.
Keep one idea per subtitle card.
Repeat key terms for retention.

## Closing
Repeat three key ideas before ending.
Invite viewers to try one mini exercise.

## Appendix A: Terminology List
- ownership: value has one clear owner at a time.
- borrowing: temporary access without moving ownership.
- mutable borrow: writable reference with exclusivity.
- immutable borrow: read-only reference with sharing.
- lifetime: scope relation that keeps references safe.
- move semantics: transfer of ownership between bindings.
- shadowing: rebind variable name with new value.
- pattern matching: branch logic through destructuring.
- trait bound: capability requirement on generic type.
- iterator: lazy sequence abstraction.
- closure: function value capturing environment.
- panic: unrecoverable runtime failure signal.
- result: explicit success or error return.
- option: explicit some or none value.
- slice: view over continuous sequence.
- module: namespace and visibility boundary.
- crate: compilation unit and package output.
- cargo: build and dependency workflow tool.
- clippy: linting helper for code quality.
- rustfmt: formatting tool for consistent style.

## Appendix B: FAQ Draft
- Q01: Why start with ownership before lifetimes.
- A01: Ownership gives the base model for all references.
- Q02: Why use many short chapters.
- A02: Short chapters reduce cognitive load and improve recall.
- Q03: Why repeat key words in every recap.
- A03: Repetition improves retention for dense topics.
- Q04: Why avoid too many metaphors.
- A04: Literal explanations reduce ambiguity.
- Q05: Why keep one action item per chapter.
- A05: One action item is easier to execute consistently.
- Q06: Why mention failure modes early.
- A06: Early failure awareness reduces frustration.
- Q07: Why include subtitle strategy in notes.
- A07: Subtitle quality directly affects comprehension speed.
- Q08: Why target calm voice style.
- A08: Calm delivery helps process complex constraints.
- Q09: Why discuss trade-offs repeatedly.
- A09: Trade-offs frame decision quality for real projects.
- Q10: Why end with a mini exercise.
- A10: Immediate practice turns passive viewing into learning.

## Appendix C: Review Checklist
- Review 01: heading hierarchy remains consistent.
- Review 02: chapter names stay concise.
- Review 03: durations are balanced.
- Review 04: vocabulary is consistent.
- Review 05: subtitle lines are readable.
- Review 06: recap bullets remain parallel.
- Review 07: transitions are short and clear.
- Review 08: examples stay practical.
- Review 09: opening goals are measurable.
- Review 10: ending summary matches goals.
- Review 11: no duplicate chapter identifiers.
- Review 12: no ambiguous pronouns in key definitions.
- Review 13: no overloaded sentences in recaps.
- Review 14: each chapter has one clear takeaway.
- Review 15: FAQ answers are direct and short.
- Review 16: terminology section has no contradictions.
- Review 17: markdown structure is stable.
- Review 18: all durations include units.
- Review 19: all bullet lists keep same style.
- Review 20: final practice prompt is actionable.

## Appendix D: 中文 English Mixed
- 中文目标: 让 grep 在 bilingual 场景下也稳定命中.
- English goal: keep regex behavior deterministic with mixed scripts.
- 中文示例: 所有权 ownership 是 rust 学习的第一关.
- English example: borrowing rules are strict but predictable.
- 中文问题: lifetime 为什么看起来复杂.
- English answer: lifetimes describe relations, not clock time.
- 中文提醒: 避免使用含糊代词, keep references explicit.
- English reminder: use short sentences for subtitle clarity.
- 中文场景: 视频章节可能同时出现 "借用" and "borrow checker".
- English scenario: chapter cards may mix zh and en keywords.
- 中文结论: 混排文本应当保持术语一致.
- English conclusion: keep term mapping stable across sections.
- 中文附注: 这部分用于测试 unicode + ascii 混合匹配.
- English note: this section helps verify mixed-language regex queries.
